sources_audited.note: The BLS article identifies NAICS 445110, reports the May 2018 49.6% combined employment share for cashiers and stock clerks/order fillers in the broader NAICS 4451/4452 population, and discusses grocery technology and productivity. The packet accurately discloses the broader, older population and the judgment required to translate occupation mix into a.

The BLS handbook lists the cited cashier duties, reports food and beverage retailers employed 29% of cashiers in 2024, and projects a 10% cashier-employment decline from 2024 to 2034, attributing the decline in part to self-service checkout and online sales.

The Census article covers BTOS data through May 3, 2026 and reports roughly 14% current and 17% expected six-month AI use in Retail Trade, with higher use at larger firm sizes. The packet correctly states that this is sector-wide business use, not grocery implementation intensity.

The 2025 Village Super Market 10-K supports the claims about intense competition, narrow margins, loyalty programs, online ordering through operator and third-party channels, regulated physical operations, and closure of an automated micro-fulfillment center. As a single public-company filing, it does not establish the prevalence of fixed-lens eligible firms or directly measure rho, q, or o; the packet treats those uses as estimates or disclosed proxies.

The FTC release reports food-and-beverage retailer revenues exceeding total costs by more than 6% in 2021 and 7% in the first three quarters of 2023 and documents allocation advantages for larger purchasers. It does not measure five-year retention of AI-created benefit, which the packet explicitly acknowledges and labels ESTIMATE.

USDA ERS reports inflation-adjusted food-at-home expenditure rising from $738 billion in 1997 to $1.10 trillion in 2025. The packet correctly labels the conversion from broad real expenditure history to five-year NAICS 445110 quantity demand as a proxy.

USDA ERS reports that 19.3% of regular U.S. grocery shoppers bought groceries online at least once in the prior 30 days in 2022 and provides frequency detail. The packet accurately discloses that incidence is neither sales share nor operator displacement and uses it only through a stated proxy bridge.

The AP article reports, citing the U.S. Census, that about 51% of small-business owners are over 55 and distinguishes internal succession, outside sale, and winding down. It provides no grocery-specific or five-year qualifying-transfer rate, so it supports only the disclosed demographic premise for the s5 proxy.

caveats: The a range is a judgmental conversion from an older, broader NAICS 4451/4452 occupation mix rather than a current six-digit wage-weighted task measure; the source population and transformation are disclosed.
The rho range extrapolates five-year implementation intensity from current sector-wide AI-use incidence and operational anecdotes, with no grocery LMM deployment-intensity observation.
The near-zero e range is not enumerated by any cited source and rests on a coherent but unverified estimate of a rare managed-service tail within a merchandise-retailing industry.
The s5 range converts a secondary, all-industry owner-age statistic into a grocery-specific five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability without observed transaction data.

The q range is an estimate: competitive conditions and pandemic-era revenue-over-cost observations do not directly measure retention of automation-created gross benefit, particularly for the rare service-led subset.
The d5 range uses broad inflation-adjusted food-at-home expenditure as a quantity proxy; it is neither a constant-quality quantity index nor specific to NAICS 445110 or the eligible service subset.

The high o range is primarily construct judgment about physical fulfillment durability; online-shopping incidence does not directly identify the share of future quantity requiring an operator in this exact NAICS or fixed lens.
summary: Mechanics pass and all eight primitive-cited sources were reopened. The record is transparent about its fixed lens, proxy transformations, estimate-heavy firm-availability inputs, and wide uncertainty. The factual source claims are accurate overall, while S4, S5, and S8 support only premises rather than the resulting primitive values. No unsupported or mislabeled input meets the methodology's materiality test: the base remains STRUCTURAL_OUT and the estimate-driven action remains VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS. The conclusion, driver, and principal weakness follow from the registered evidence, so the record is publishable with caveats.
